title: Srimad Bhagavatam | HG Chanchalapathi Dasa | SB 3.28.8 | 14-10-2016
speaker: HG Chanchalapathi Dasa

segments.text: Sitting in an easy posture is called swasti samasinaha.
It is recommended in the yoga scripture that one should put the soles of the feet between the two thighs and ankles and sit straight.
That posture will help one to concentrate his mind on the Supreme Personality of Godhead.
This very process is also recommended in Bhagavad Gita 6th chapter.
It is further suggested that one sit in a secluded, sanctified spot.
The seat should consist of deerskin and kusha grass topped with cotton.
So we are reading these scriptures.
These verses from the Srimad Bhagavatam spoken by Kapiladeva to his mother Devahuti.
And here he is describing the process of dhyana, meditation.
And very similar to what is described in the Bhagavad Gita.
One should go to a shuchaudeshe.
Shuchidesha means a sacred place, a sanctified place.
And sitting in an easy posture.
And in this context Srila Prabhupada mentions in the Bhagavad Gita.
Immediately refers to going to a holy place, a sanctified place.
Like Rishikesh, Haridwar, banks of Ganges, banks of Yamuna.
And says it does not refer to modern cities.
So in other words Prabhupada points out that this dhyana process.
Even in our country in the past we hear about.
In the Puranas we hear about kings retiring from the kingdom.
They didn't decide to stay in the kingdom itself somewhere.
But they went to the Himalayas.
They went to some holy place.
Which will help them control their minds.
Because that is the process that is recommended in dhyana.
And not suitable for people living in modern cities.
Because this whole process requires another kind of.
This is a different kind of an approach.
In the Bhagavatam there are many places this kind of a dhyana is mentioned.
Like here Kapila is telling Devahuti.
To do this kind of a dhyana.
Also going to a holy place.
And Dhruva went to a holy place in Vrindavan.
So many places this dhyana is mentioned.
What the Vedic literatures are talking about.
What is with relation to bhakti?
In the beginning of this chapter Prabhupada mentioned that.
Because it is one of the Vaishnava disciplines.
So how do we understand all of that?
And also in another place in the same canto.
By and large it is like this.
Of approach in the absolute truth.
To come in touch with the absolute truth.
The association or coming in touch with the absolute truth.
And a understanding of the absolute truth.
Like the understanding that I am a person.
I am a person living here in this world.
On the existence of my parents.
So my existence is a relative existence.
My parents existence is also relative.
On the existence of their parents.
Which is not dependent on anything else.
So that's the purpose of the Vedic literatures.
Mentioned all over in the Vedic literatures.
An overview of all of this is presented.
Direct method of approaching the absolute truth.
And there is an indirect method of approaching the absolute truth.
First let's understand what is the.
There is a certain amount of detachment.
some of those karmas and all of that there is a lot of karma kanda aspect in that so by and large

detachment understanding of brahman realization of paramatma and realization of bhagavan which
is the complete realization so this is a gradual step-by-step process the other process is directly
aiming towards Krishna by offering the results to Krishna working for Krishna practicing some
regulated bhakti and that bhakti regulated bhakti
leads to pure bhakti and by pure bhakti one understands complete absolute truth so these
are the two methods that are recommended but for Kali Yuga what Chaitanya Mahaprabhu points
out is that the indirect method is very risky one cannot practice Varanashrama properly one
very attached to the sense gratification opportunities that are offered in the
process and hence when Vyasa Deva had compiled all of these Vedic literatures when he met Narada
and when he was feeling that something I have not done completely and then when Narada explains he
says that this indirect method that you have recommended and this indirect method is very risky
By and large, in all of the Vedic literatures, it is a jugupsitam mahanvyatikramaha. It is simply an abominable, condemnable process. And it is a great mistake, blunder you have committed.

And then Narada advises him to write about the direct process and that's how the Bhagavatam comes about. So Bhagavatam stresses the direct process and Chaitanya Mahaprabhu presenting that talks about the direct process rather than the indirect process.

The direct process of dhyana, some of these are mentioned in the Vedic literature, in the Srimad Bhagavatam. Because dhyana, for instance, is also about understanding the Paramatma and transferring to Vaikunthaloka. It's almost there at the very high level.
Because varnashrama, dhyana, dhyana and then bhakti, you are almost there. But the dhyana process...

The dhyana process is not practical for this age, especially for the people living in the cities. Because it says, shucaudeshe pratishtapya. You have to go to a sacred place. And hence this is not very practical.
The other method is, one can offer the results of one's activities and work for Krishna. And you can do that process in any circumstance.
Including a circumstance like somebody is in a battlefield like Arjuna was in. He was able to fight because it was Krishna's work. And doing Krishna's work is that stage.
So like this, the two methods have been recommended. And Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's recommendation is that one should follow the direct process.
Another example of how the indirect process works.
Another example of how the indirect process works.
Another example of how the indirect process works.
Another example of how the indirect process fails is the practice of Karmakanda by the people.
Because Karmakanda allows a certain amount of Debbie God worship and offering meet in a regulated way. And that is alone.
And hence when people try to practice that at that certain state.
They became so attached to eating meat and killing animals and they converted all the temples into slaughterhouses and when people questioned them, how are you killing animals?
See it is given in the Vedas, we can kill the animals and so at that time Lord had to appear as Buddha in order to teach the principle of Ahimsa.
So this is also explained by Narada and in the Narada Muni and Vyasa Devas conversation. When Narada said that Jagup Siddham, what you have done is abominable.
So Vyasa Deva said no, no, no, I have also put some regulations. I have said that you can enjoy but in a regulated way.
But people in Kali Yuga where Rajogunar,
I have also put some regulations. I have said that you can enjoy but in a regulated way. But people in Kali Yuga where Rajogunar,
Tamoguna are prominent, they will be so carried away by the spirit of enjoyment that is permitted in the Karmakanta.
Tasya nivaranam, tasya manyate nivaranam janah. Na manyate nivaranam janah.
Narada Muni says they will hardly care for your regulations, restrictions.
Exactly as it happened and Lord Buddha had to appear in order to set right the thing.
is filled with risks and may not result in the ultimate realization of the absolute truth.
And also it may take many, many lifetimes of progress.
The indirect process is a slow process.
The direct process on the other hand is practicing regulated bhakti, sadhana bhakti.
offering the results of our activities to Krishna directly, and in this way approaching the Absolute Truth, which is the direct process.
And now this direct process was referred to by Krishna in the conversation, was emphasized in the Bhagavad Gita.

So, Bhagavad Gita, although it is Mahabharata, it is describing all the intrigues between the Pandavas and the Kauravas, and all that is happening, and Krishna involved in some of these things, all that is explained.
In the middle of that, Krishna's dialogue, conversation, Krishna's instructions to Arjuna is full of the direct process, and not the indirect process.

Right in the second chapter, Krishna is referring, there is another indirect process, and Veda, Vada, Ratha, Apartha, these people who are followers of the Vedas, they follow that, and they think that this is the highest thing.
Boga, Ishvarya, Gatimprati, to make progress on the path of Boga and Aishwarya, that's the highest thing.
Nanya, Dasti, Diva, Dinaha, and they consider this is the highest thing.
There are people like that, that's referring to the indirect process.
But then Krishna says, no, no, there is another process, Boga, Ishvarya, Prasaktanam, Taya, Apartha, Shetasam, Vyavasayatmika, Buddhi, Samadha, Unavidhyate.
That Samadhi, complete absorption in the absolute truth, is not possible for those who are attached to the path of enjoyment and opulence, Boga, Ishvarya, Gatimprati.
So, he begins to tell, and finally, in the 18th chapter, Krishna is saying,
What is that all Dharma Parityajya he is saying?

I have found one mistake in Bhagavad Gita.
I said, oh really? What is this Bhagavad Gita?
No, so many Acharyas have written commentaries on Gita, nobody has said.

in order to establish Dharma Aikam,
but later on Krishna is saying,

he should be giving up Adharma, that should have been the thing.

knowing that there are different kinds of people in the society...

and they all were coming up with different ideas there was a lot of

some were saying no no you should not do that some were saying no no you should not do that
some were saying no no you should not do that some were saying no no you should not do that
you should do something else and there was confused at that time Shukadeva came
and Shukadeva knew the bhagavatam and he spoke and everyone's became silent and
said yes what he is saying that's the ultimate thing suitable for a great
person like Pariksit and there that conversation happened so like this there
were many many occasions where Yama Raj spoke to Yama Dootas when the Yama Raj

Yamadūtas tried to conquer Ajāmila and they could not. Some other strange personalities came and stopped them and they went back to Yamarāja and asked, what is this happening? And then Yamarāja told them, the bhāgavata knowledge.

So like this, Vyāsadeva compiles all those instances where the bhāgavata knowledge, the direct process was explained by great personalities and all of those he compiled together and formed the bhāgavatam.
And this is the essence of all the Vedic literatures and this is most suitable for Kali Yuga and this process has to be practiced.
Now for the direct process to work, one or two important features are required.

If you take all of these examples, when Śukadeva was instructed by Vedavyasa, Parīkṣit was instructed by Śukadeva, the sages of Naimishāraṇyā were instructed by Suta Gosvāmī or Yamadūtas were instructed by Yamarāja.
And so many, Dhruva was instructed by Nārada. Like that, many, many examples if you take.

In all of these, for the bhāgavata process, the direct quick process to work, one important feature is, you must come in touch with a pure devotee who knows Kṛṣṇa, who has that intimate understanding of Kṛṣṇa.
That is the trigger for the direct process.
And so bhāgavatam process for it to work, we have to come in touch with a pure devotee and serve a pure devotee.
And another important feature for the bhāgavata process to work, which is an accelerated process, direct process, and that requirement is to chant the holy name of the Lord,
to come in touch with the Lord directly.
And for Kali Yuga, the Lord has appeared in the form of holy name and to chant the holy name.
This whole thing is described in a very succinct manner.
In this purport, I will read this one.
As mentioned in the previous verses, there are two kinds of devotional service.
The way of regulated principles and the way of full attachment in love to the Supreme Personality of Godhead.
This is referring to the ultimate process.
There are two kinds of devotional service.
The way of regulated principles and the way of full attachment.
The full attachment is having full bhakti, highest level of prema bhakti.
If you cannot do that, to achieve that, you have to do regulated bhakti.
For those who are actually not able to follow the principles of Kṛṣṇa consciousness, it is better to cultivate knowledge,
because by knowledge, one can be able to understand his real position.
Gradually, knowledge will develop to the point of meditation.
By meditation, one can be able to understand the Supreme Personality of Godhead by a gradual process.
There are processes which make one understand that one himself is the Supreme,
and that sort of meditation is preferred.
One is unable to engage in devotional service.
If one is not able to meditate in such a way,
then there are prescribed duties as enjoined in the Vedic literature,
for the brāhmaṇas, vaiśyas, śūdras, which we shall find later in the chapter of Bhagavad-gītā.
Now coming back to the karma-pala-tyāga.
One should give up the results of chapter of Bhagavad-gītā.
But in all cases, one should give up the result or fruits of labor.
This means to employ the result of karma for some good cause,
In summary, to reach the Supreme Personality of Godhead,
the highest goal, there are two processes.
One process is by gradual development, and the other process is direct.
Devotional service in Kṛṣṇa consciousness is the direct method.
And the other method involves renouncing the fruits of one's activities.
Then one can come to the stage of knowledge,
then to the stage of meditation,
then to the stage of understanding the Supersoul,
and then to the stage of the Supreme Personality of Godhead.
One may either take the step-by-step process or the indirect path.
The direct process is not possible for everyone.
Therefore, the indirect process is also good.
It is, however, to be understood that the indirect process is not recommended for Arjuna,
because he is already a saint.
He is already at the stage of loving devotional service to the Supreme Lord.
It is for others who are not at this stage.
For them, the gradual process of renunciation, knowledge, meditation,
and realization of the Supersoul and Brahman should be followed.
But as far as Bhagavad-gītā is concerned, it is the direct method that is stressed.
Everyone is advised to take to the direct method
and surrender unto the Supreme Personality of Godhead, Kṛṣṇa.
So, this is the process that is recommended by Caitanya Mahāprabhu.
And this is the process that the entire Bhagavatam is all about.
How to take the direct process?
And that was further summarized by Caitanya Mahāprabhu as Sādhanabhakti.
And that leads to Rāgānuga Bhakti.
And as we advance in Rāgānuga Bhakti,
we come to the highest stage.
The highest stages of Prema Bhakti.
One will have complete understanding of the Absolute Truth.
So, this is the overall picture that we get.
And so, in that sense, we see that Dhyāna,
Dhyāna was recommended, like Kapila is mentioning,
because it was more suitable for other ages.
and all of these examples are there
because it was suitable for another age.
And one could make gradual progress
and then the ability to transfer to Vaikuṇṭha Loka.
another aspect that was also mentioned
is to disclose the highest aspects of Krishna
and His personality, His nature, and His activities.
And Krishna had just then appeared,
And so, Vyāsadeva wanted to disclose
And among them, the Vraja activities,
those are considered even more exalted
because there He exhibited the Madhurya Bhakti,
And so, those are also very, very confidential aspects
and Vyāsadeva explains all of that.
So, like this, Chaitanya Mahāprabhu helps us
to get an overview of the whole Vedic process,
where all these things fall into place,
and what is the position of Bhakti,
what is the position of the Bhagavatam process.
Grantharāja Śrīmad Bhāgavatam ki Śrīla Prabhupāda ki.
